coredata.dc:title: Inner filter with carbon quantum dots: A selective sensing platform for detection of hematin in human red cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Qian Qian
Chen, Bin Bin
Zou, Hong Yan
Li, Yuan Fang
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Hematin plays a crucial role in various physiological functions, and the determination of hematin in complex biological matrixes is a significant but difficult issue. Considering the unique photophysical/photochemical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared with p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) and ethanol, a new strategy for the design of fluorescent probes for hematin has been achieved. The proposed sensor array is fabricated based on the inner filter effect (IFE) between hematin and CQDs with phenomenon of selective fluorescence quenching of CQDs which results from the strong absorption of the excitation and emission spectrum of CQDs by hematin. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs is closely related to the amount of hematin and there is a good linear relationship over the range of 0.5–10μM with a detection limit of 0.25μM. What's more, the fluorescence assay has been successfully applied for hematin sensing in healthy human red cells showing this sensing assay has a great potential prospect for detection of hematin in the complex matrixes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Hematin
Fluorescent probes
Inner filter effect
Human red cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterizations of the carbon quantum dots
The stability of the as-prepared CQDs
Determination of hematin in healthy man red cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CQDs. (a) TEM image of CQDs (inset: particle size distribution of CQDs; (b) HRTEM of the CQDs.

Optical and bioactive properties of the CQDs. (a) Emission spectra; (b) UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of CQDs. UV–Vis spectra (black line), excitation spectra (blue line) and emission spectra (red line) (Inset: CQDs solution under the 365nm UV lights lamp.); (c) Cellular toxicity of CQDs on Hep-2 cell viability; (d) cellular images at the bright field (A), cellular images by excitation at the DAPI channel (B), GFP475 channel (C), and the merged images (D).
The elemental and structure analysis of CQDs. (a) XPS; (b) XPS C1s; (c) FTIR and (d) Raman spectrum of CQDs.

Sensitivity of hematin detection. (a) Fluorescence intensity of CQDs with different concentrations of hematin (0.5, 0.75, 1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100μM; c CQDs, 0.05mg/mL, excitation wavelength, 350nm, pH, 7.4 BR buffer); (b) The linear relationship between F 0/F and different concentrations of hematin (c CQDs, 0.05mg/mL, excitation wavelength, 350nm, emission wavelength, 460nm, pH, 7.4 BR buffer).

The mechanism of fluorescence quenching of CQDs by hematin. (a) UV–vis absorption spectra of hematin and the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs; (b) the time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) of CQDs alone and CQDs with different concentrations of hematin (5μM, 500nM, 100nM); (c) The cyclic voltammetry curve of CQDs; (d) Energy levels of the LUMO and HOMO of CQDs and hematin.

Fluorescence responses of the CQDs at 460nm (c CQDs, 0.05mg/mL, excitation wavelength, 350nm, emission wavelength, 460nm, pH, 7.4, BR buffer) with different interfering molecules and irons (500μM, blue bars) and in the presence of hematin (50μM, red bars).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of CQDs using p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) by solvethermal method and the detection process of hematin.
Determination of hematin in healthy man red cells.

head.ce:title: Inner filter with carbon quantum dots: A selective sensing platform for detection of hematin in human red cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analysis (Southwest University), Chongqing Science & Technology Commission, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analysis (Southwest University), Chongqing Science & Technology Commission, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hematin plays a crucial role in various physiological functions, and the determination of hematin in complex biological matrixes is a significant but difficult issue. Considering the unique photophysical/photochemical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) prepared with p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) and ethanol, a new strategy for the design of fluorescent probes for hematin has been achieved. The proposed sensor array is fabricated based on the inner filter effect (IFE) between hematin and CQDs with phenomenon of selective fluorescence quenching of CQDs which results from the strong absorption of the excitation and emission spectrum of CQDs by hematin. The fluorescence quenching of CQDs is closely related to the amount of hematin and there is a good linear relationship over the range of 0.5–10μM with a detection limit of 0.25μM. What's more, the fluorescence assay has been successfully applied for hematin sensing in healthy human red cells showing this sensing assay has a great potential prospect for detection of hematin in the complex matrixes.

ce:section.ce:para: Hematin, Fe3+ protoporphyrin IX liganded to hydroxyl, is known as the oxidized form of heme, which is the prosthetic group of proteins such as hemoglobin and myoglobin (Light and Olson, 1990). When released from hemoglobin to the red cell, heme, Fe2+ protoporphyrin IX, would turn to hematin. Hematin is proven to be a toxic substance in vivo. On one hand, it triggers lyses of human erythrocytes (Chou and Fitch, 1981; Kirschner-Zilber et al., 1982; Levin et al., 1990) and other cell types (Fitch et al., 1983; Goldstein et al., 2003) and catalyses the generation of toxic lipid peroxidation products by several mechanisms (Dutra et al., 2014), generating redox reactive substances such as H2O2, superoxide radicals, and the hydroxyl radical, which directly mediates lipid peroxidation (Becker et al., 2004; Klouche et al., 2004). On the other hand, hematin level could up regulate under pathological conditions and lead to various inflammatory lesions including vascular disorders, renal failure, and immunemediated disorders (Buehler and Agnillo, 2010; Kumar and Bandyopadhyay, 2005; Tolosano et al., 1999). Hematin binds to the red cell membrane causing membrane damage (Chen et al., 2011) and higher adhesion of sickle red cell to leucocytes and the endothelium and related sickle-cell anaemia (Ishop et al., 2011; Larsen et al., 2010; Li et al., 2003). Considering the toxicity of hematin, it is very significant to establish an easy, fast and cheap method to detect the hematin. Enormous work such as mass spectrometry (Whiteaker et al., 2004), absorption spectrophotometry (Fitch et al., 1983; Huy et al., 2005), electrochemical method (Scheller et al., 2005; Tan and Hua, 2001), HPLC (Crouser et al., 2003) and fluorescent spectrometry (Pang et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2011) have been developed, but mainly focused on the determination of the total heme in the red cell. Note that the free heme is found in the form of hematin because the dissociation of heme from ferrous hemoglobin is extremely slow and heme mostly dissociates from methemoglobin in the form of hematin. As a result, the determination of hematin has a great problem.

To meet the challenges above, people have tried to develop new strategies for determination of hematin recently. Aich et al. established an enzyme-catalyzed chemiluminescence method to determine the concentration of hematin in healthy human erythrocytes (Aich et al., 2015). Li et al. (2014) proposed a colorimetric method for sensing hematin in the cerebral system based on the rational design of G-quadruplex DNAzymes. Although the two well-established methods for determination of hematin were realized, they were both time-consuming and required tedious procedures to assay with expensive instruments. Ji et al. reported an approach for assaying the concentration of hematin with boron-doped graphene quantum dots (BGQDs) based on the electron transfer mechanism (Ji et al., 2016). However, the synthesis of the BGQDs was partly complicated by carefully controlling the voltage and synthesis environment. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are known to exhibit unique optical properties as fluorescent probe in bioanalytical field for their good water solubility, high photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility (Jigna et al., 2017; Vaibhavkumar et al., 2015, 2014a, 2014b).

Herein, we propose a selective assay for hematin quantitation based on the inner filter effect resulting from the absorption spectrum overlaps of hematin with the excitation and emission spectrum of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) easily prepared by p-aminobenzoic acid (PABA) and ethanol. The results show that it is the high molar coefficient of hematin that makes the photoluminescence of CQDs quenching with the high quenching constant, and thus resulting in a high selectivity from interferes in human red cells. Importantly, the practical application of the developed strategy is demonstrated by employing this probe to detect hematin in human blood samples, which indicates that this work has a great potential prospect for quantitation of hematin in the complex matrixes.

P-aminobenzocic acid and ethanol are commercially obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and Kelong Chemical Group Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). AA, L-Cys, GSH, L-Gly are all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium chloride (NaCl) and a Britton–Robinson (BR) buffer are adopted to adjust the ionic strength and control the acidity, respectively. All solutions are prepared with deionized water from a Millipore Milli-Q Ultrapure Water System.

The UV absorption spectra of CQDs and hematin are gained from a Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The elemental composition of CQDs is measured with an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. The HRTEM data of CQDs are performed on a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The FTIR spectrum of CQDs is collected on a Hitachi FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of CQDs are recorded with a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The Raman spectrum of CQDs on the AgNPs solution is scanned through a LabRAM HR800 Laser confocal Raman spectrometer. The fluorescence lifetime of CQDs are measured with a FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). The fluorescence imaging is conducted using a DSU live-cell confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan).

The CQDs are prepared through a simple solvethermal method. 100mg p-aminobenzocic acid and 5mL ethanol are added into a 25mL Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 180°C for 12h. Next, the autoclave is naturally cooled down to room temperature and we collect the resulted yellow liquid. Then, in order to separate the unreacted P-aminobenzocic acid and ethanol, the silica column chromatography has been used.

Human red cell samples are collected from three healthy male and female adult volunteers from our laboratory. To prepare the hemolysate, we add 45mL of isotonic 0.9% NaCl solution to 4mL of blood sample and then gently agitate the tube to homogenize the suspension and centrifuge the diluted blood for 20min. The supernatant are decanted. After washing three times by the above procedures, the red blood cells are suspended to 50mL of deionized water. The red cells rupture under the influence of the osmotic pressure difference between the cells and the water. The free heme as hematin is released from the human red cells. We then centrifuge this suspension to compress the cell membranes at the tube bottom, collect the hemolysate, and store it at 5°C in a sterile tube for further analysis. Then, the hemolysate was diluted with 100-fold ultrapure water before detection ( Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image of CQDs ( Fig. 1a) shows that the resulting CQDs are well mono-dispersed with average diameter of 11.8nm (Fig. 1a insert). The HRTEM image of CQDs (Fig. 1b) clearly indicates the lattice spacing of 0.21nm and 0.31nm, which may be attributed to the (100 facet) of graphitic carbon (Qu et al., 2016) and the spacing between graphene layers (002 facet) (Lin et al., 2016), respectively. They have the typical absorption at 282nm which is ascribed to the typical absorption of π-π* transition of the aromatic system ( Fig. 2a) (Liu et al., 2016). Excitation dependent emission (EDE) property is also observed with the excitation wavelength ranging from 350nm to 450nm (Fig. 2b) (Liu et al., 2017a, 2017b), different from the excitation independent emission (EIE), high probably due to the wide ranged size distribution (Fig. 1a). And the maximum emission is at 460nm with the excitation at 350nm. So, under the 365nm UV lights lamp, the CQDs solution shows a brightly blue green fluorescence emission (inset in Fig. 2a). The bioactive properties of CQDs are also investigated. It shows that the cytotoxicity of CQDs is very low (Fig. 2c), and the viability of Hep-2 cells remains greater than 85% even if incubated with a relatively high concentration (0.01mg/mL) for 24h. Furthermore, Hep-2 cells could be clearly observed at the bright view (Fig. 2d A), and a bright green and blue scene could be seen in the cytoplasmic area (Fig. 2d B, C), and the Fig. 2d D indicates the merged of Fig. 2d B and Fig. 2d C of a bright cyan, suggesting that the as-prepared CQDs could be used for cell imaging with a high resolution.

Moreover, the XPS ( Fig. 3a) is used to reveal the surface elements and chemical bonds of CQDs. It indicates that CQDs are composed of C, N and O atoms, with the C atom of 71.05%, O atom of 25.2% and N atom of 3.75% (Fig. 3a). Three peaks, at 284.8, 399.3, and 533.1eV, are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. And the high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s (Fig. 3b, Fig. S1, ESI†) further confirm the presence of C–C, C–N, C˭O, C–OH, and C–N–C bonds in CQDs (Chen et al., 2016). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 3c) is used to identify the functional groups in CQDs. The spectrum of CQDs exhibits the -νC-H bands in the 2854 and 2920cm−1 regions, as well as the νO-H, νC˭O and νC-O bands at 3448, 1641 and 1083cm−1, confirming the presence of hydroxyl, amide, amino and carboxyl group (Chen et al., 2016, 2015; Liu et al., 2017a, 2017b). The Raman spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows that the D-band at 1381cm−1 is due to sp3 carbons, while the G-band at 1610cm−1 is due to sp2 carbons (Huang et al., 2013) Above all, the results suggested that the as-prepared CQDs could be used for cytoplasmic staining, labelling and targeting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the as-prepared CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared CQDs show excellent photostability for the fluorescence intensity remains invariant under continuous excitation (350nm) with a UV lamp for 150min (Fig. S2, ESI†). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs slightly changes even if the concentrations of NaCl and H2O2 reach as high as 1M and 500μM respectively (Fig. S3, ESI†), indicating that the CQDs can be very stable in a medium with comparatively high ionic strength or under the oxidation condition. These results indicate that CQDs have good stability and great potential toward designing fluorescence nanoprobe for the complex matrixes.

Fluorescence titrations are conducted to evaluate the sensitivity of the CQDs fluorescent nanoprobe for hematin. Under the optimal conditions (pH 7.4, Fig. S4, ESI†), the fluorescence emission of the as-prepared CQDs is found to be quenched by hematin with the characteristic emission red-shift ( Fig. 4a), and the fluorescence quenching of the CQDs at 460nm is closely related to the amount of hematin and there is a good linear relationship between fluorescence quenching of CQDs and hematin over the range of 0.5μM–10μM (Fig. 4b) with a detection limit of 0.25μM. And the reproducibility and repeatability are determined at the same operating condition during six days (Fig. S5) and repeated six times for 10μM hematin (Fig. S6). The RSDs are 2.1% and 1.2%, which indicates the methods have the good reproducibility and repeatability. Moreover, the Stern–Volmer's model (Lin et al., 2016) was used to model the quenching kinetics of CQDs with hematin.
F
0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
c
q
= 1 +
K
q
τ
0
c
q
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the fluorophores in the absence and in the presence of a quencher respectively; c q is the concentration of the quencher, K sv is the dynamic quenching constant, and in this case K sv = 0.08μM−1 by taking the slope of the regression line in Fig. 4b. K q is the quenching rate constant and τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs (τ 0 = 5.89ns). F 0/F displays the good linear relationship with the Stern–Volmer plot. The resulted K q has the value of 1.17 × 1013 M−1s−1, suggesting a static quenching rather than a dynamic quenching in accordance that the rate constant of a dynamic quenching is usually less than 1.0 × 1010 M−1 s−1 (Chen et al., 2016; Lin et al., 2016).

To explore the sensing mechanism, a series of experiments have been carried out. Usually, the spectral overlapping between the absorption band of the hematin and the excitation or emission band of the CQDs ( Fig. 5a) suggests that the fluorescence modulation might be related to the energy transfer, IFE process, or electron transfer (Chen et al., 2014; Ji et al., 2016; Jiri et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2014; Yu et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2015). The lifetime of the CQDs in the absence and presence of 5μM, 500nM, 50nM hematin remains almost the same (Fig. 5b), there is no energy transfer process between CQDs and hematin (Lin et al., 2016).

To estimate the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) energy levels of CQDs, Cyclic voltammetry (CV) has been carried out by using a standard three electrode system, which consists of platinum sheet as the working electrode, a Pt-wire counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. By using cyclic voltammetry with the potential sweeping between −1.0 and 1.0V at a scan rate of 0.5Vs−1, ECL signals of the CQDs (0.1mg/mL) are recorded in the electrolyte containing phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (0.1M, pH 7.4), and the reduction potential for CQDs is determined to be −0.88eV (Fig. 5c). The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of CQDs are calculated according to the following equations (Zhang et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2013)
(1)
E
H O M O
= − e
(
E
o x
+ 4.4
)
(
e V
)
(2)
E
L U M O
= − e
(
E
r e d
+
4.4
)
(
e V
)
Where E ox and E red are onset of oxidation and reduction potential for CQDs respectively. So the corresponding LUMO level is −3.52eV. However, the HOMO energy could not be obtained due to the irreversible of the oxidation behavior (data not shown). The HOMO level could be obtained according to the following equation (E g resulting from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum):
(3)
E
H O M O
=
E
L U M O
–
E
g
E g is estimated to be 4.21eV. So, the E HOMO of CQDs is calculated to be −7.73eV.

Meanwhile, the E HOMO and E LUMO of hematin could be calculated as −2.15eV and −5.02eV (Ji et al., 2016). Apparently, as shown in Fig. 5d, it is impossible for the electron in the LUMO of the CQDs to transfer to the LUMO of the hematin.

As both the energy transfer and electron transfer process are excluded, the IFE appears to be more favoured for sufficient spectral overlaps between the absorption band of the hematin and the excitation and emission band of the CQDs, especially when hematin has a high molar extinction coefficient (ε = 54,200μML−1 cm−1) at 386nm which can result in high IFE efficiency.

The fluorescence response specificity of CQDs toward hematin over other molecules and ions in complex biological matrixes has been investigated. After the solution of CQDs is treated with different molecules and ions, the fluorescence intensities at 460nm are measured. As shown in Fig. 6, significant decreases in the fluorescence intensity are after CQDs are reacted with hematin, whereas no obvious changes in emission are detected after CQDs react with other molecules or ions even with ten times concentrations. Competition experiments are then conducted, in which hematin and other molecules or ions are added into the CQDs solution. In this case, the fluorescence intensity is decreased to the comparable level of CQDs solution reacted with hematin only. And a high molar extinction coefficient of hematin at 386nm compared to the other interferents in human blood samples increases the probability of CQDs detecting hematin selectively (Fig. S7, Table S1). Therefore, these results clearly indicate that CQDs can be used as a probe to discriminate hematin in some complex solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hematin in healthy man red cells
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method is then applied in the detection of hematin in healthy man red cells. The reliability and accuracy of the detection method we have developed are evaluated through the recovery study (Ali et al., 2015; Feng et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2017), which is carried out on the samples spiked with standard hematin solutions (1, 5, 10μM). The obtained recoveries of three samples varied from 98.9% to 107.0%, which indicates that this work has a great application prospect in hematin simplicity and specificity detection ( Table 1).

In conclusion, a selective fluorescence assay for the detection of hematin was designed based on the inner filter effect between with hematin and carbon quantum dots. The high molar coefficient of hematin results in a high selectivity from various interferes in human red cells. Furthermore, this new sensing platform has been successfully used for the selective detection of hematin in the human red cells, which indicates that this work has a great potential application prospect in the complex biological matrixes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant no. 21535006), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2016C177) and Doctoral Scientific Research Foundation (SWU116006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.08.049.
